steps.title: Map the structure
steps.content: The text has a clear three-part structure. First, it identifies the problem: the north celestial pole is a fixed point around which stars appear to rotate, but it is discernible only at night. Second, it introduces the source of the researchers' idea: some insects and birds use skylight polarization for navigation. Third, it explains the devised method: a polarimetric camera captures changing polarization patterns so daytime latitude and bearing relative to the pole can be determined.

steps.title: Reject added claims
steps.content: Choice A adds claims about most navigational tools and says animals navigate without using the pole, neither of which the text states. Choice B turns the passage into a comparison of two methods and their accuracy, but no such assessment appears. Choice C says the text contrasts how humans and animals use the pole and suggests improving existing instruments, which goes beyond the passage. Choice D follows the actual order: obstacle, animal-inspired solution, and optical-device method. The answer is D.
